definition: The structural-versus-cyclical classification of rate divergence is the critical first-order analytical question. Across 8 historical episodes: structural divergences (different economic regimes) produced 25-50% FX moves over 2-4 years; cyclical divergences (timing differences) produced 10-20% moves reverting in 12-18 months. Current configuration is mixed: ECB leg likely cyclical, BoJ leg possibly structural.
evidence: 8 historical episodes classified with consistent magnitude-duration separation
Structural: 25-50% moves over 2-4 years; cyclical: 10-20% reverting in 12-18 months
Judged 'the most important analytical contribution' in pair_0 debate
Generalist_01 skipped this classification entirely — identified as a gap
Three-variable discriminant function applied to 8 historical episodes
open_questions: Can structural vs cyclical be classified in real-time with sufficient confidence to act on?
What diagnostic signals distinguish structural from cyclical during the first 6 months?
Specific FX magnitude ranges propagate too much uncertainty from the 75%-accurate classifier
Whether additional discriminant variables would improve accuracy
Directional distinction survives but quantitative precision does not
